title: Assignment 1
text:
My first assignment has 4 parts.


(Veri Bilimi ve Endüstri Mühendisliği Üzerine Sohbetler - Kerem Demirtaş & Erdi Dasdemir) named video is chosen.



Open Ended Question:
Question:
In the data science workflow why is problem definition considered the most critical first step? Explain using the “Survivorship Bias”.
Answer:
Problem definition is critical because solving the wrong problem leads to incorrect conclusions even if the analysis is technically correct. In World War II where analysts examined bullet holes on returning bombers and suggested reinforcing the areas where most holes appeared. However, statistician Abraham Wald realized that the data only came from planes that survived. The planes that were hit in other areas (such as the engine or cockpit) never returned. Therefore, reinforcing the already damaged areas was incorrect; the vulnerable areas with no bullet holes on surviving planes were actually the places that needed reinforcement. This example demonstrates that correctly framing the problem determines whether the analysis leads to useful decisions.
Multiple-Choice Question
Which of the following correctly describes the typical stages of a data science project?
A. Data collection → Model building → Publish results → End of project
B. Problem definition → Data collection and preparation → Exploratory data analysis → Model building → Evaluation → Deployment
C. Model building → Data visualization → Data collection → Deployment
D. Data collection → Artificial intelligence training → Automation → Deployment
Correct Answer:
B. Problem definition → Data collection and preparation → Exploratory data analysis → Model building → Evaluation → Deployment
{Before I checked and rewrite small parts of the questions in this assignment I gathered the transcript from the chosen video and then fed to chatgpt using this prompt:
Using this file help me on this: “Prepare one open-ended question and one multiple-choice question, along with their answers.”}



After feeding the assignment file into chatgpt I used the following prompt at first:
“Write a single piece of code for parts 3c and 3d.”
After I get an error I used this prompt as a followup:
“I get this error in Replace NA loop: Warning in [<-.factor(tmp, is.na(new_data[[col]]), value =”Fatih_18”) : invalid factor level, NA generated Error in charToDate(x) : character string is not in a standard unambiguous format”

# -------------------------------
# PART 3c
# Statistical summaries with mtcars
# -------------------------------

compute_stats <- function(x){
  c(
    Mean = mean(x),
    Median = median(x),
    Variance = var(x),
    IQR = IQR(x),
    Min = min(x),
    Max = max(x)
  )
}

cat("\n=================================\n")


=================================

cat("PART 3c — mtcars Statistical Summary\n")

PART 3c — mtcars Statistical Summary

cat("=================================\n\n")

=================================

data(mtcars)

# For-loop approach
stats_table <- sapply(mtcars, compute_stats)
stats_table <- round(stats_table, 3)

cat("Summary statistics for each column:\n\n")

Summary statistics for each column:

print(stats_table)

            4178             4178             1409             1409 
           grade       samplesize            state        startdate 
             429                1                0                0 

# Create new dataset
new_data <- polls_us_election_2016

# Replace NA values safely
for (col in names(new_data)) {
  
  if (is.numeric(new_data[[col]])) {
    
    new_data[[col]][is.na(new_data[[col]])] <- my_birth_year + k
    
  } else if (inherits(new_data[[col]], "Date")) {
    
    new_data[[col]][is.na(new_data[[col]])] <- Sys.Date()
    
  } else {
    
    new_data[[col]] <- as.character(new_data[[col]])
    new_data[[col]][is.na(new_data[[col]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
    
  }
}

# Display rows of new dataset
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  print(head(new_data, k))
} else {
  print(tail(new_data, k))
}

title: Assignment 1
text:
Open Ended Question:
Question:
In the data science workflow why is problem definition considered the most critical first step? Explain using the “Survivorship Bias”.
Answer:
Problem definition is critical because solving the wrong problem leads to incorrect conclusions even if the analysis is technically correct. In World War II where analysts examined bullet holes on returning bombers and suggested reinforcing the areas where most holes appeared. However, statistician Abraham Wald realized that the data only came from planes that survived. The planes that were hit in other areas (such as the engine or cockpit) never returned. Therefore, reinforcing the already damaged areas was incorrect; the vulnerable areas with no bullet holes on surviving planes were actually the places that needed reinforcement. This example demonstrates that correctly framing the problem determines whether the analysis leads to useful decisions.

Which of the following correctly describes the typical stages of a data science project?
A. Data collection → Model building → Publish results → End of project
B. Problem definition → Data collection and preparation → Exploratory data analysis → Model building → Evaluation → Deployment
C. Model building → Data visualization → Data collection → Deployment
D. Data collection → Artificial intelligence training → Automation → Deployment
Correct Answer:
B. Problem definition → Data collection and preparation → Exploratory data analysis → Model building → Evaluation → Deployment

# Create new dataset
new_data <- polls_us_election_2016

# Replace NA values safely
for (col in names(new_data)) {
  
  if (is.numeric(new_data[[col]])) {
    
    new_data[[col]][is.na(new_data[[col]])] <- my_birth_year + k
    
  } else if (inherits(new_data[[col]], "Date")) {
    
    new_data[[col]][is.na(new_data[[col]])] <- Sys.Date()
    
  } else {
    
    new_data[[col]] <- as.character(new_data[[col]])
    new_data[[col]][is.na(new_data[[col]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
    
  }
}

# Display rows of new dataset
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  print(head(new_data, k))
} else {
  print(tail(new_data, k))
}
